1.2 Dot Product Applications

  1. One of the first fundamental applications of dot product is converting between lengths and angles and coordinates.

    Example 1.2.1.

    Consider points Find

    Solution of Example 1.2.1. Express in terms of vectors, and . Therefore, , . Thus,

    Example 1.2.2 (Pset 1 Part II p1).

    Find the dihedral angle between two faces of a regular tetrahedron.

    Solution. Since all of the dihedral angles are equal (regular tetrahedron), we only need to find one such angle.

    Method 1: Fix one face as the base and have its center to be the origin in the space while it lies in the x-y plane with one vertex at (0, 1, 0).

    Then the desired angle could be computed as Example 1.2.1.

    Method 2: Notice the dihedral angle between two planes is equal to the angle between their normal vectors, we only need to compute the angle between two normal vectors.

    Thus, the angle could be computed with Theorem 1.1.3.

  2. Dot product is pivotal in dealing with surfaces.

    Example 1.2.3.

    Fix , show that the surface defined by is a plane.

    Solution of Example 1.2.3. Notice that the surface definition is

    Geometrically, this is all vectors that are orthogonal to a fixed vector , therefore a plane.

    Corollary 1.2.3.1.

    For a plane defined by , it’s normal vector is .

    This result could be generalized to surfaces beyond planes, See Theorem 2.6.2 and Theorem 2.2.2.

    Theorem 1.2.4.

    Three planes intersect at a point iff their normal vectors are coplanar.

    Proof.

    Left as an exercise.

  3. In Physics, when dealing with force, dot product is often critical.

    Example 1.2.5.

    Background: By carefully positioning the sail the boat can be made to sail into the wind – this process is called tacking.

    Described mathematically, the wind vector is first projected onto the perpendicular to the sail to obtain the direction of the force on the sail. This resultant force is then projected onto the axis of the boat to determine the direction in which the boat is being pushed. By orienting the sail correctly, this double projection can result in a vector with a component pointing into the wind.

    In the figure, is the wind direction. The line is perpendicular to the sail , and the line lies along the boat’s axis .

    a) Let be the projection of onto the line . Show that does not have a non-zero component in the direction opposite . (It is sufficient to show the projections on the sketch.)

    b) Find the projection of onto . (Give an explicit formula in terms of and .) What is the condition on and that this projection has a component in the direction? (For warmup you might try the specific case .)

    Solution. (a) omitted due to simplicity. (b) The magnitude is

    while the direction is along geometrically, there is a component iff
